specification.topic-statement: The scrollback layout system computes and maintains the terminal row count for each stored text line, recalculating that count arithmetically — without rescanning text — when the terminal width changes.

scope.in-scope: Buffer initialization, line appending with layout computation, terminal resize reflow, logical-line-to-physical-row lookup, and line text retrieval.
scope.boundary: This specification covers which physical rows a line occupies. The computation of which bytes of a line correspond to a specific physical row — used when rendering a partial line during scrolling — is a separate concern.
Allocation failure causes the process to abort immediately. No error is returned to the caller.

field.name: terminal width
field.note: cached value of the last width used for layout computation; used to detect when reflow is needed

field.name: total physical rows
field.note: cached sum of all stored lines' physical row counts

field.name: segment widths
field.note: one entry per newline-delimited segment; entry count equals newline count plus one; immutable after initial computation

field.name: display width
field.note: sum of all segment widths; immutable after initial computation

field.name: physical row count
field.note: recalculated when terminal width changes; always at least one

field.name: newline count
field.note: count of embedded newline characters; immutable after initial computation

behavior.buffer-initialization: A new scrollback buffer is created with an initial capacity for 16 lines and 1024 bytes of text storage. No lines are stored. The supplied terminal width is recorded as the current width. The cached total physical row count is zero.
behavior.line-appending:
A logical line is added by supplying its text as a byte sequence. The text is stored verbatim, then a layout is computed for the line.
The text bytes are appended to a contiguous text store. If the store cannot hold the incoming bytes plus a null terminator, its capacity is doubled repeatedly until it can. An offset into the text store and a byte length are recorded for the new line.
Once grown, the text store never releases that capacity, even after lines are removed.
A single pass over the text is performed to compute the line's layout. The text is divided into segments at each embedded newline character. For each segment, the display width is computed as the sum of the display widths of its characters.
ANSI CSI escape sequences are detected and consumed entirely without contributing any display width. Newline characters serve as segment boundaries and contribute no display width to any segment. Each valid Unicode codepoint contributes the number of display columns defined by the Unicode character width tables for that codepoint. Each invalid UTF-8 byte contributes exactly one display column.
The display width of each segment is stored in an array. The array has exactly one more entry than the line's newline count. The stored segment widths are immutable and never modified after this initial computation. The sum of all segment widths is also stored as the line's total display width.
The number of physical terminal rows the line occupies is derived from the segment widths and the current terminal width.
For each segment whose display width is greater than zero, the row count for that segment is the smallest integer greater than or equal to the segment's display width divided by the terminal width (ceiling division).
For each segment whose display width is zero, the row count for that segment is exactly one.
The line's total physical row count is the sum of the row counts for all segments.
A line with no text has one segment with zero display width. That segment contributes one row, so an empty line always occupies exactly one physical row.
A line ending with a newline character has an empty trailing segment with zero display width. That segment contributes one row. The text "hello\n" therefore occupies two physical rows at any terminal width wide enough to hold "hello" on a single row.
Every empty segment between consecutive newlines contributes one row. The text "A\n\nB" produces three segments — "A", "", and "B" — and occupies three physical rows at any terminal width of at least one column.
The new line's physical row count is added to the buffer's cached total physical row count.
If the line array is at capacity, it is doubled before storing the new entry. The text offset and length arrays are grown in parallel to maintain the same capacity.
behavior.terminal-resize:
When a terminal width is supplied to ensure the layout is current, the supplied width is compared to the cached terminal width.
If the supplied width equals the cached width, the operation returns immediately. No computation is performed.
When the supplied width differs from the cached width, the physical row count of every stored line is recalculated using its stored segment widths and the new terminal width. The same rules apply: zero-width segments contribute one row; positive-width segments contribute the ceiling of their display width divided by the new terminal width.
The text bytes are not read during reflow. Only the stored, immutable segment widths are used.
The cached total physical row count and the cached terminal width are updated to reflect the new values.
No Unicode decoding or ANSI sequence detection occurs during terminal resize. All per-segment display widths were captured at append time. Reflow performs only integer arithmetic on those stored values.
behavior.physical-row-lookup:
A physical row number (zero-based from the top of the buffer) is mapped to a logical line index and a row offset within that line.
The buffer is scanned from the first line. For each line, its physical row count is accumulated into a running total. When the running total first exceeds the requested physical row number, that line is the result. The row offset within the line is the requested row number minus the accumulated total of all preceding lines.
If the requested physical row number is greater than or equal to the buffer's total physical row count, the lookup fails.
behavior.line-text-retrieval:
A logical line's stored text is retrieved by its zero-based line index. The operation returns a pointer into the text store at the line's recorded offset, along with the line's recorded byte length. The returned pointer remains valid until the buffer is next modified.
If the supplied index equals or exceeds the number of stored lines, the retrieval fails.
behavior.buffer-clear: All stored lines and their layout data are discarded. The line count, text byte usage, and total physical row count are reset to zero. The allocated capacity of the line arrays and the text store is preserved and not released.
behavior.buffer-copy: All lines from a source buffer are appended to a destination buffer one by one in order. Each line undergoes a full layout computation against the destination buffer's current terminal width. The source buffer is not modified.
